definition: A 'void' contract is treated as if it never existed and has no legal effect. An 'unenforceable' contract may have been validly formed but cannot be enforced by a court. The distinction matters because voidness traditionally carries 'customary incidents' including the right to go to court for rescission, while unenforceability is typically raised as a defense.
examples: The 1980 amendments replaced 'shall be void' with 'unenforceable by either party,' which petitioners argue eliminated the basis for the TAMA rescission right.
